title: Rust: Fearless System programming
summary: A witty, concept-first tour of Rust for the agentic-coding era — ownership, illegal-states-unrepresentable, errors as values, fearless concurrency, zero-cost abstractions, Cargo, how to build Rust with a coding agent, and when Rust is (and isn't) the right call.

concepts.title: Lets work on a Rust Programming book that will excite readers!. It should be witty, funny. You can do your research to create a Rust Programming book that is relevant to this Agentic LLM coding world. What I mean by that is, developers/readers  are not going to be interested in Syntax. Instead, we should focus on concepts. Once readers understand the concept, they will be able to use agents like Claude code to write Rust applications. We should include some scenarios where Rust will be apt for developing solutions.
The Borrow Checker Is a Pair Programmer
Where Rust Is the Right Call

images.prompt: A striking, modern editorial illustration in flat vector style: a stylized crab (a nod to Rust's mascot, Ferris) forged out of metal — its left half glowing molten orange like poured steel, its right half cooled into solid graphite-blue iron, with a small bright-green checkmark stamped into the cooled shell. A faint forge/blueprint grid in the background, warm rust-orange + iron-blue palette with one green accent, generous whitespace, soft depth, no text baked in. Mood: raw power made trustworthy.
images.alt: A crab-shaped ingot of metal, half molten orange and half cooled iron, stamped with a green checkmark
images.caption: Rust: raw, fast metal — cooled into something the compiler will vouch for.
